title: Critical Water Resources Prioritization Act of 2025
short_title: Critical Water Resources Prioritization Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill changes the law about protecting endangered animals and plants to help water agencies get water when people really need it. If a water agency needs water for drinking, emergencies, keeping people healthy, or growing food, they can ask to skip some of the animal protection rules for up to six months. To get this special permission, the water agency has to show they tried other ways to get water and have a plan to protect the endangered animals and plants. They can ask for more time if they still need it after six months.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Critical Water Resources Prioritization Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Critical Water Resources Exemptions Under the Endangered Species Act of 1973
sections.text: This section changes the Endangered Species Act of nineteen seventy three by adding new rules about water use. The law adds a new part called Human Needs Exemptions for Water Resources. First, it defines important terms. A critical human water need means water that is required for drinking water supplies in cities and towns, for emergency services like fighting fires, to keep people healthy and safe as decided by the Secretary or a water management agency, or for food security as decided by the Secretary. A water management agency means any federal, state, or local agency that has the power to manage or divide up water resources. The law then explains when agencies can get exemptions from normal Endangered Species Act rules. The Secretary can allow a water management agency to skip the usual consultation requirements if those requirements would directly stop the agency from meeting a critical human water need. To get an exemption, the water management agency must show they have used all reasonable ways to save water, looked at all possible other water sources, and decided no other reasonable options exist to meet the critical human water need. The agency must also give the Secretary paperwork proving these conditions exist, a plan to reduce harm to affected species, and a timeline for going back to following the normal requirements. Each exemption lasts for no more than one hundred eighty days. The Secretary can renew an exemption for additional one hundred eighty day periods if the same conditions still exist and the water management agency submits the required information again. Agencies with exemptions must send monthly reports to the Secretary describing the ongoing critical human water need, efforts to find other water sources, impacts on affected species, and steps taken to reduce those impacts. The Secretary must send Congress a yearly report describing all exemptions made, the total impact of those exemptions on affected species, and recommendations for better balancing critical human water needs with species protection. Any action taken under these new rules can only be reviewed by courts to see if the agency acted in an arbitrary and capricious way under section seven hundred six of title five of the United States Code. The Secretary must create regulations to put these changes into effect within one hundred eighty days after this law is passed.
